text: Hello friends, I am Dr. Sanjoy Mandal, GI surgeon from Kolkata and today we will be talking about a rare topic called Achillesia Cardia. Now this is a disease where a patient essentially presents with difficulty in swallowing. Now let me explain a bit about the disease and for those who do not know, I'll go down a bit into the basics. Now as you can see here, this is the esophagus through which we swallow our food and below is the stomach. At the junction of the esophagus and the stomach is what we call the lower esophageal sphincter. When we swallow food, this sphincter relaxes and allows food from the esophagus to pass into the stomach. But it prevents food which is already in the stomach from refluxing back into the esophagus. So what happens in achillesia cardia? To put it very simply, there is a neurological problem which prevents this lower esophageal sphincter from relaxing. And this ultimately leads to food not being able to pass from the esophagus to the stomach. So as a treatment for this disease, We operate on these patients and we cut this abnormal lower esophageal sphincter which then allows the food to easily pass down. But a downside to this procedure is that food can reflux back into the esophagus. And to prevent this from occurring, we add an anti-reflux procedure to this.

The surgeon uses a liver retractor through the epigastric port to elevate the left lobe of the liver. Upon entering the abdominal cavity, the short gastric vessels are taken down using a Ligasure energy device, aiding in the mobilization of the hiatus and facilitating a floppy fundus for potential anti-reflux procedures.

The surgeon carefully mobilizes the fundus, avoiding injury to the spleen to prevent bleeding complications. After taking down the short gastric vessels, the hiatus is mobilized from the left crura. The surgeon then moves to the anterior aspect of the gastroesophageal junction for complete mobilization, followed by incising the lesser omentum to approach the right side of the junction.

The surgeon begins dissection on the right side to expose the right crus, aiming for a complete 360-degree mobilization of the hiatus. Care is taken to avoid injury to the vagus nerves. The dissection involves both blunt and sharp techniques, including a posterior hiatal dissection to fully mobilize the gastroesophageal junction.

The surgeon begins the cardiomyotomy after completing hiatal mobilization and exposing the gastroesophageal junction. Using a Maryland dissector, the muscle fibers are gently parted to expose the inner circular muscle layer. Bleeding or oozing is controlled by dabbing with a gauze piece, avoiding excessive use of energy sources to prevent mucosal injury. The inner circular muscle layer is carefully divided, ensuring the mucosa remains intact.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to separate the esophageal mucosa from the circular muscle layer. Monopolar diathermy is employed to cut the circular muscles, revealing the mucosa, which visibly pouts out. Bleeding points are identified and precisely cauterized. The myotomy extends at least five centimeters proximally on the esophagus and three centimeters distally on the stomach. Blunt dissection further separates the mucosa from the muscle, followed by muscle transection using a ligature device.

Air insufflation is performed to assess mucosal integrity and confirm the completion of the myotomy. Further myotomy is conducted on the stomach side, followed by a repeat air insufflation test, which shows air passing easily into the stomach, indicating intact mucosa.

The surgeon creates a partial anterior wrap using a patch of stomach to cover the prolapsing mucosa, securing it with three-point suture fixation at the left margin, apex, and right margin of the myotomy. The apex suture is tied, followed by the third suture between the stomach and the right margin. The widened hiatus is then narrowed to prevent stomach herniation into the thoracic cavity.
